messages.content: You are a senior research assistant. The user will paste a document. Summarise it in detail with structured sections.

Document follows. Please summarise.

The transformer architecture, introduced in the paper 'Attention Is All You Need' (Vaswani et al., 2017), revolutionised natural language processing by replacing recurrent layers with self-attention mechanisms. Unlike RNNs which process sequences sequentially, transformers compute attention across all token pairs in parallel, enabling massive parallelisation on GPUs. The original paper demonstrated state-of-the-art results on English-to-German and English-to-French translation tasks, training in a fraction of the time required by recurrent baselines. The architecture consists of an encoder stack and a decoder stack, each containing alternating multi-head attention and feed-forward sub-layers, with residual connections and layer normalisation around every sub-layer. Multi-head attention splits the model dimension into h heads, applies scaled dot-product attention independently, and concatenates results — letting the model attend to information from different representation subspaces. Positional encodings, added to the input embeddings, give the model a sense of token order since attention itself is permutation-invariant. The decoder additionally uses masked self-attention to prevent leftward information flow during training, and cross-attention layers that attend to the encoder's output. Subsequent work — BERT (Devlin et al., 2018), GPT (Radford et al.), T5 (Raffel et al., 2019), and many others — built on this foundation, scaling models to hundreds of billions of parameters and pretraining on terabyte-scale corpora. The success of scaling laws (Kaplan et al., 2020; Hoffmann et al., 2022) suggested that performance improves predictably with model size, dataset size, and compute. More recently, the focus has shifted toward instruction tuning, reinforcement learning from human feedback (Christiano et al., 2017; Ouyang et al., 2022), and tool use. The decoder-only transformer remains the dominant architecture for large language models, though efficiency-focused variants (mixture-of-experts, state-space models like Mamba) are an active research area. Please produce a comprehensive summary covering the architecture, key innovations, scaling results, and the lineage of follow-up work.
